claim: Reasoning models benefit from a minimum baseline of model capability in System 1 (base model capability) to be effective; applying reasoning to very small models like GPT-2 would provide almost no lift.
actionable: When evaluating whether test-time compute investments will pay off for a task, first ensure your base model meets a capability threshold; below that threshold, additional inference-time reasoning yields minimal returns.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Tasks where success is easily verifiable (math, coding) are not the only domains where reasoning models excel; Deep Research (generating research reports on complex topics) demonstrates reasoning models succeed in non-verifiable domains with subjective quality measures.
actionable: When designing benchmarks for reasoning models, don't restrict yourself to easily-verifiable tasks; create fuzzy-task evaluations where humans can judge output quality even if there's no ground truth answer.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Harnesses and scaffolding (tool calls, model routers, check-legality functions) are temporary crutches that get replaced by raw model capability; don't architect long-term systems around them as they'll be obsoleted by future model scale.
actionable: When building agentic systems, use harnesses to unblock near-term product velocity, but design incrementally so scaffolding can be removed as models improve; avoid investing 6 months in infrastructure that will be washed away by scale in 3 months.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Distinguishing test-time compute from outcome-based feedback loops: test-time compute is when the model simulates internally (e.g., checking if a chess move is legal before taking it); outcome feedback is when the model takes a real action, gets feedback it failed, then backtracks—the latter doesn't scale to real-world robotics or irreversible actions.
actionable: When designing agent task loops, provide tool calls for checking validity (test-time compute friendly) rather than relying on the agent to attempt actions and recover from failures; the latter breaks in real-world embodied scenarios.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) on domain-specific data creates a data asset that complements model scaling; unlike harnesses that get obsoleted, RFT-collected data remains useful as future models improve and can be fine-tuned again on newer models.
actionable: Prioritize building reward models and collecting RFT data for your domain now; this investment compounds as models scale, unlike scaffolding which gets replaced.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Game-theoretic reasoning about model router design: a dumb/fast router can still recognize when a problem is hard and route to a capable model, but risks overconfidence and being fooled—there's a real speed-vs-accuracy tradeoff that will eventually be solved by improving the base model rather than smarter routing logic.
actionable: If building a model router today for quick wins, do it; but recognize the tradeoff and plan to migrate to a single, more capable model as soon as possible rather than optimizing the routing heuristic.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Visual reasoning tasks (tic-tac-toe, GeoGuessr) benefit from System 2 (test-time compute) because they contain solvable spatial puzzles with all info available; pure information retrieval tasks (memorized facts) do not benefit from longer thinking because you either know it or you don't.
actionable: When choosing whether to enable test-time compute for a user-facing task, assess whether the task involves reasoning over available info (do enable) or pure recall (disable to reduce latency).
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Self-play converges to minimax equilibrium in two-player zero-sum games (chess, Go, poker heads-up) but becomes an ill-defined optimization problem in non-zero-sum games (diplomacy, multi-agent collaboration, math reasoning); the AlphaGo analogy breaks down outside that narrow domain.
actionable: When considering self-play for multi-agent training, first verify you have a well-defined objective function; if optimizing for collaboration or open-ended tasks, self-play alone won't work—you need explicit objective design.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Implicit world models emerge through model scaling; explicit world modeling is not necessary because sufficiently large LLMs develop theory of mind and world understanding implicitly as a byproduct of capability improvements.
actionable: Stop building explicit world model modules for LLM agents; invest in base model capability instead and let world modeling emerge implicitly.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: The primary limit of test-time compute scaling is cost (spending budget) and wall-clock time for iteration: as models think for hours/days, experiment iteration cycles become serial and slow, bottlenecking the research feedback loop more severely than inference cost.
actionable: When planning test-time compute scaling, budget for parallelization and offline evaluation infrastructure; don't assume you can iterate as quickly on 3-hour-thinking models as on instant-response models.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Humans are dramatically more data-efficient than current LLMs; a human learns from ~5 examples while LLMs require ~200 examples per data point—this gap is one of the most important unsolved research questions and may be solvable via algorithmic improvements or by collecting qualitatively different training data (not just internet text).
actionable: If working on data efficiency, consider both algorithmic innovations and data source diversity; internet-scale data is convenient but not the ceiling of what's learnable.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Reasoning models (O3) can perform general research work (e.g., acting as a mini deep research tool producing reports in 3 minutes) with sufficient quality to replace or augment human expert manual research for non-verifiable domains.
actionable: For knowledge-work products involving research, writing, or synthesis of sources, consider deploying reasoning models for first-pass generation to increase throughput; users can then refine outputs.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Hallucinations in language models (contradicting earlier statements in the same conversation, inventing facts) are becoming less frequent as model scale increases; hallucination detection is therefore becoming harder because people can no longer reliably spot bots by looking for weird consistency violations.
actionable: When designing human-AI collaboration systems, don't rely on hallucination frequency as a safety signal or user-detection mechanism; better models will pass the Turing test more reliably.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Poker AI optimization became stuck on game-theory-optimal (GTO) strategies because they guarantee not losing but don't maximize exploitation of weaker opponents; the unsolved problem is building exploitative poker AIs that adapt to opponent weaknesses—a sample efficiency challenge since AIs need 10,000 hands to profile opponents vs. humans needing ~12.
actionable: If building competitive AI systems, recognize the GTO-vs-exploitation tradeoff; for zero-sum games GTO is a local maximum, but for collaborative or hierarchical scenarios, exploitative opponent-modeling becomes critical.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Raw pre-trained language models (before mid-training and post-training) are near-useless for practical tasks due to mode collapse and poor instruction-following; all deployed models go through mid-training (adding task-specific data) then post-training (RLHF-style alignment) before release.
actionable: When evaluating open-source models, assess their post-training quality, not raw pretraining capability; raw models won't perform tasks as intended.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: OpenAI succeeded with the scaling paradigm partly because of organizational structure: operating as a startup (not academia or research lab) allowed pooling compute resources and making hard allocation decisions; other labs are now copying this structure.
actionable: If leading AI research, adopt startup-like resource allocation and collaboration models; traditional academic departmental silos slow paradigm shifts.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Benchmarks that only admit easily-verifiable answers (multiple-choice, exact math solutions) limit evaluation scope and can't measure progress on fuzzy tasks (writing quality, research depth, design trade-offs); the field over-indexes on the easy-to-grade paradigm.
actionable: Build human-judged benchmarks for fuzzy tasks even if scoring is expensive; this unblocks evaluation on tasks that matter but aren't multiple-choice.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI

claim: Game selection for AI research should prioritize problems where the objective function is hard to define (Magic: The Gathering, Stratego with massive hidden state) as these challenge reasoning generality more than perfectly-solvable games; but game-specific techniques become less valuable as general reasoning improves.
actionable: When choosing a game benchmark, opt for unsolved-objective games if you're researching general reasoning; game-specific algorithm improvements have diminishing returns.
source_title: Scaling Test Time Compute to Multi-Agent Civilizations — Noam Brown, OpenAI
